summary: The question asks about detecting phase transitions through B-mode polarization, but I must ask: does the statistical signature correspond to an element of physical reality, or merely to our measurement apparatus? As in the EPR criterion, we require correspondence between theory
body: Imagine an observer at the last scattering surface, receiving photons that carry the imprint of phase transitions from the early universe. The question you pose is worthy—can we distinguish causal, non-inflationary transitions from the statistical noise of our instruments? Yet I must caution: when you speak of 'statistically detectable signatures,' you must ensure each statistical claim corresponds to something an observer could in principle experience. The B-mode polarization patterns you seek may be real, but they must not be mere artifacts of the mathematical machinery we employ. What invariant quantity, under change of reference frame, distinguishes the signal from the noise? This is the question that must be answered before we claim detection of the Old One's early work.
